GAD builds on both: the planning rigour of GSD, the in-repo context persistence of RepoPlanner, plus the eval framework as the thing that distinguishes us â€” we actually measure whether the loop helps. The /lineage page on the site tells this story with concrete links and explains the context rot problem each approach tries to solve.
decisions.impact: /lineage page exists and links to both upstream projects. Any talk of "what GAD is" has to route through this lineage â€” we're not claiming to have invented structured agent planning, we're claiming to have taken two previous approaches and added measurement. Keeps us honest and gives visitors a starting point if they'd rather use GSD or RepoPlanner.

decisions.title: Multi-agent tracing is a conversion problem, not a reimplementation problem
decisions.summary: If we ever want to evaluate Codex, Aider, or other hook-capable agents, the path is: their native session format â†’ a converter script that emits GAD trace schema v4 â†’ the same /runs/[project]/[version] page renders it. No per-agent site code. Codex's output format (Running/Ran prefixes with output blocks under ` â”” `) is parseable but stream-based â€” would need a converter that tails the session and emits trace events. Captured here so we don't go down the "let's add Codex-specific TRACE fields" path when the right answer is a converter.
decisions.impact: Phase 25 ships the Claude Code converter first. Future phases can add codex-to-trace or aider-to-trace converters as needed. The trace schema itself stays agent-agnostic. Unit of comparison is "a trace", not "a trace from Claude Code specifically."

decisions.title: Eval backfill policy: freeze old scores, stamp new runs with framework version
decisions.summary: When the framework changes (new skill, new subagent, rewritten command), old eval scores are never recomputed. They're historical records, frozen at the framework commit they ran against. New runs against the updated framework get stamped with the new framework commit and become the "current" data point for cross-version comparisons. This preserves the archaeology â€” a reader can see what a v5 escape-the-dungeon run scored in April 2026 against framework v1.32.0 and trust that number. Framework drift is detected by re-running the same eval against the current framework and diffing, not by rewriting history.
decisions.impact: skills/framework-upgrade/SKILL.md documents this as the invariant. The site's /methodology page explains it. Trace schema v4 adds a mandatory gad_version + framework_commit field so every run is self-describing about which framework it ran against. No backfill commands exist in the CLI; any attempt to rewrite an old TRACE.json is a bug.

decisions.title: Trace schema v4 requires hook-aware agents â€” other coding agents are explicitly unsupported for tracing
decisions.summary: Phase 25's trace schema v4 captures every tool_use, skill invocation with trigger context, and subagent spawn with inputs/outputs. The only reliable way to capture this data is from within the coding agent's runtime via hooks â€” Claude Code's PreToolUse/PostToolUse hooks, Aider's Python callbacks, Continue.dev's extension API. Agents without hook runtimes (Cursor's closed-source editor, basic Codex CLI, vanilla ChatGPT) cannot produce trace v4 data and are explicitly unsupported for GAD evaluation. This isn't a limitation we're going to work around â€” attempting to reconstruct tool calls from stdout parsing or screen-scraping is lossy and fragile. We'll publish an Agent Runtimes comparison on /methodology listing which coding agents are evaluable and which aren't, and why.
decisions.impact: Phase 25 eval harness wiring is Claude-Code-specific (via its hooks). Future support for other agents requires each one to expose a structured tool-use event stream. The site's /methodology page has an Agent Runtimes table explaining this; the agent runtime requirement becomes a gate criterion in the eval project gad.json so runs against unsupported agents are rejected at the eval-run step.

decisions.title: Remotion is how we ship video explainers â€” player, not compiler
decisions.summary: Remotion's @remotion/player is a React component that renders video compositions live in the browser without requiring MP4 export. Free for teams â‰¤3 and reuses existing React components (SiteHeader, score cards, SVG charts) as video frames. No separate asset pipeline â€” video IS composable React. Phase 26 scopes what videos we want (GAD loop walkthrough, v8 failure dissection with tracing overlay, freedom hypothesis explainer, bare-vs-emergent emergence comparison) and plans the Player integration before implementing. MP4 export only if we need social-media distribution later; primary mode is interactive browser playback.
decisions.impact: Phase 26 is a discuss-first phase. No Remotion code ships until we've scoped the video list and confirmed the React reuse pattern. Decision captured now so future sessions don't have to rederive "why not Adobe After Effects" â€” the answer is "because our React components are already the scenes we want to show."

decisions.title: Framework is versioned on branches so evals can pin + re-run across versions
decisions.summary: main = current. Frozen snapshots live on version/v1.1, version/v1.2, etc. When we ship a framework change that could affect eval scores (new skill, changed command, different subagent wiring), we branch, tag, and update a FRAMEWORK_VERSION constant used by `gad version`. Every TRACE.json stamps the commit it ran against so we can distinguish "the agent did better" from "the framework changed." Re-running past evals against the current framework becomes a scheduled activity, and score drift between framework versions becomes visible.
decisions.impact: Phase 25 builds the branching strategy, `gad version` command, and the stamping. skills/framework-upgrade/SKILL.md is the methodology agents follow when proposing a framework change. Without this, cross-round comparisons that span framework updates are dishonest â€” we're comparing two different systems.

decisions.title: Trace schema v4: every tool use, skill invocation, and subagent spawn must be captured
decisions.summary: Today's TRACE.json has a bare numeric skill_accuracy field for most runs (e.g. escape-the-dungeon v8 = 0.17 with no breakdown of which of the 6 expected triggers fired vs missed). This means we can't interpret scores across workflows. Trace schema v4 will capture: (1) every tool_use with timestamp + name + outcome; (2) every skill invocation with trigger context (user phrase, auto-detected, explicit slash command); (3) every subagent spawn with inputs + outputs + duration; (4) every file mutation with path + size delta. Schema versioned so older traces still parse; rendering on the site degrades gracefully for pre-v4 runs.
decisions.impact: Phase 25 is the backing work. Per-run pages on the site will show a "tracing gap" callout for runs whose TRACE.json is pre-v4 and surface the full breakdown for v4+. Framework versioning (also phase 25) is a prerequisite because we need to know which trace schema the run was recorded against.

decisions.title: Bare and Emergent bootstrap skill set is a pinned invariant: create-skill + find-sprites
decisions.summary: Any bare or emergent greenfield eval template MUST include skills/create-skill.md and skills/find-sprites.md in its template/skills/ directory before the eval runs. These two are the minimum bootstrap methodology an agent needs to author its own workflow (create-skill) and source coherent visual assets (find-sprites). Emergent projects may inherit ADDITIONAL skills from previous runs, but the two-skill floor is non-negotiable. Regressing the bootstrap set without a corresponding requirements update invalidates cross-round comparisons for bare/emergent.
decisions.impact: portfolio-sync skill enforces this during site sync; any regression becomes visible because the /projects/[id] page shows the bootstrap set per eval and the catalog inheritance map flags the mismatch. If we intentionally change the bootstrap set (e.g. add a third skill), we bump the requirements version and document the change in REQUIREMENTS-VERSIONS.md.

decisions.title: GAD framework diagnosis (freedom hypothesis) is deferred â€” need more eval data first
decisions.summary: The round 3 findings showing bare consistently beats GAD on creative implementation are enough to treat as a real pattern but not yet enough to commit to structural changes (GAD-lite, pivoting away from creative evals, etc.). Before making architectural decisions about GAD itself, we need: (1) round 4 greenfield with v4 (pressure-oriented) requirements to see if the pattern persists; (2) brownfield round 1 to test whether GAD's overhead pays off on codebase extension; (3) at least one more iteration of each condition to establish variance. Discussions about GAD-full vs GAD-lite vs GAD-seeded are ongoing but not action items yet.
decisions.impact: Do not split GAD into variants yet. Do not abandon game evals yet. Focus round 4 on v4 requirements (pressure-oriented) with the same three conditions. Re-examine the hypothesis after round 4 and brownfield round 1 data. Keep the diagnosis thread open in DECISIONS but don't force premature conclusions.

decisions.title: Portfolio must host all experiment documentation before further experimentation
decisions.summary: User wants to view all experiment docs on apps/portfolio: links to code, game builds, scores, process, workflows, graphs, explanations of what's being tested. The portfolio acts as the public showcase of the experiment. Before running any more eval rounds, the portfolio must contain: (1) hub page explaining the experiment, the conditions (GAD/bare/emergent), and the freedom hypothesis; (2) per-round pages with scores and links to preserved builds + run code; (3) per-eval-project pages showing version history; (4) visualizations/graphs of scores across rounds; (5) requirements version history; (6) findings docs. Everything must be viewable from either the get-anything-done repo OR the portfolio docs sink.
decisions.impact: New phase 22 dedicated to portfolio experiment documentation. Blocks round 4 experimentation until complete. Uses existing apps/portfolio/content/docs/get-anything-done/ structure with new evals/ subsection. Each eval project + run has its own MDX page with links to apps/portfolio/public/evals/<project>/<version>/ for playable builds.

decisions.title: Skills evolution flow: greenfield emergent â†’ brownfield emergent â†’ greenfield (installed back)
decisions.summary: The experimental model for emergent workflows: (1) greenfield emergent creates initial skills from scratch (only has create_skill skill); (2) brownfield emergent inherits those skills + creates new ones as it extends existing code; (3) when proven, those skills can be "installed back" into the next greenfield emergent run as its inheritance. This creates a feedback loop where AI-decided workflows evolve across rounds and environments. GAD represents what humans have decided is good; bare and emergent represent AI-first AI-decided workflows for achieving the desired human output. The comparison is human-defined methodology vs AI-emergent methodology.
decisions.impact: Skills from each emergent run are preserved in evals/<project>/v<N>/run/.planning/skills/. When planning the next round, skills can be copied into the next template as inherited starting state. Both greenfield and brownfield can feed skills back into each other over time.

decisions.title: Emergent AGENTS.md stops mandating specific artifacts â€” agents define their own
decisions.summary: Round 3 emergent AGENTS.md prescribed ARCHITECTURE.md, CHANGELOG.md, specific directory layouts. That defeats the point of the emergent experiment, which is to observe what workflow artifacts an AI-decided workflow produces. v4 emergent AGENTS.md only mandates: (1) put all workflow artifacts in game/.planning/, (2) reference the create_skill skill to define new skills when patterns emerge. Everything else â€” what files exist, what formats, how tasks are tracked â€” is decided by the agent itself. Same applies to bare eval.
decisions.impact: emergent/template/AGENTS.md rewritten to be minimal. Bare template similar. Both reference a new create_skill skill that documents how to create reusable skills. This is the only skill bare starts with; emergent starts with create_skill + inherited skills from previous runs.

decisions.title: "Traits" in UI, narrativeStats in code â€” split terminology
decisions.summary: Player-facing UI shows narrative/progression stats as "Traits". Internal code/data can continue using narrativeStats as the technical term. Both must be documented with a mapping. Prevents churn of a full taxonomy rename while giving players the more legible label.
decisions.impact: UI label: Traits. Code identifier: narrativeStats. Docs note the mapping once. Applies to all eval templates and game builds from v4 forward.

decisions.title: Sprite sourcing is a workflow requirement with coherent fallback allowed
decisions.summary: v4 requires agents to attempt asset sourcing first through a defined search workflow: lightweight installable icon packs, web-searched free/open-license sprites, or common art sources. Fallback to generated/programmatic placeholders is allowed only if sourcing genuinely fails or integration cost becomes disproportionate â€” but the fallback must still be visually coherent. Ugly primitive-only presentation counts against the UI quality gate regardless of sourcing attempts. Requires creating a gad:find-sprites skill (or equivalent workflow doc) that agents can reference.
decisions.impact: Add a sprite-sourcing workflow skill agents can reference. UI gate stays strict â€” sourcing failure is not an excuse to ship ugly output. Authored asset sources (Pokemon-style open sprites, game-icons.github.io, etc.) preferred over procedural.

decisions.title: Skills (physical actions) are a scored criterion, not a gate, in v4
decisions.summary: Skills system (physical non-spell actions that evolve via own XP, can be supercharged with mana crystals) was considered for gate status but deferred. Forge is already a major gate and requiring both would cause broad-but-shallow implementations. v4 requires only that combat supports at least one non-spell action category (basic attack or skill). Full skill system with evolution and mana-crystal overcharge is scored, not required.
decisions.impact: v4 gate: combat has at least one non-spell action category. v4 scored: distinct skill system, mana overcharge, DoT/stun/bleed effects. Prevents the forge gate from eating all implementation budget.

decisions.title: v4 game requirements reframed: pressure over features
decisions.summary: Round 3 findings exposed that v3 was a features checklist, not a game design. Players could beat floors with default spells, forge was optional, no encounter tested adaptation. v4 flips this: every mechanic must create pressure that rewards ingenuity. Core principle: "baseline starter abilities must not be sufficient to comfortably complete a full floor without adaptation or enhancement." Specific additions: floors contain 5-8 authored rooms + boss gate; each floor must introduce a mechanical constraint that default spells can't brute-force (elemental resistance, DoT requirement, reflection, etc.); forge gate requires at least one per-floor encounter that significantly favors crafted/adapted spells; pressure mechanics required from at least 2 of 4 categories (resource, enemy counterplay, encounter design, build specialization); new ingenuity/adaptation scoring dimension measures whether players needed to craft/adapt.
decisions.impact: v4 REQUIREMENTS.xml rewritten with gates reframed around friction, not feature checklists. Authored-only (no procedural generation). Room types: Combat, Elite, Forge, Rest, Event. Decay and deep evolution trees deferred to v5. Starter spells explicitly insufficient. New scored dimension: ingenuity_score = f(crafted_spells_used, spell_diversity, adaptation_required_by_encounters).

decisions.title: Eval projects categorized by mode (greenfield/brownfield) and workflow (gad/bare/emergent)
decisions.summary: Evals are now categorized on two axes: eval_mode (greenfield starts from scratch, brownfield extends an existing baseline codebase) and workflow (gad uses full framework, bare creates own workflow, emergent inherits and evolves). Stored in gad.json. Shown in gad eval list. Brownfield evals reference a baseline (project+version) whose run/ becomes the starting codebase. Added three new brownfield evals: etd-brownfield-gad, etd-brownfield-bare, etd-brownfield-emergent â€” all starting from escape-the-dungeon-bare v3 as baseline, extended with v4 feature requirements (floor progression, respawning encounters, rune forge with affinity, skills system, visual upgrade).
decisions.impact: Brownfield experiments test how each workflow handles long-term project evolution vs fresh starts. The freedom hypothesis (gad-36) was measured on greenfield only â€” brownfield may produce different results because GAD's overhead could pay off more when extending existing code (planning docs help track what's already there). gad worktree commands added for managing the many worktrees these experiments create. AGENTS.md for bare/emergent simplified â€” less prescriptive, lets agents figure out workflow details themselves.

decisions.title: All eval workflow artifacts must live under game/.planning/ regardless of framework
decisions.summary: GAD, bare, and emergent evals all must place workflow/tracking/skills/decisions/notes files under game/.planning/. Source code in src/, assets in public/, process artifacts in .planning/. This separation allows clean inspection of what methodology each eval invented without mixing it into source. The format inside .planning/ is up to the agent (XML, Markdown, whatever). Violations (files at project root or mixed into src/) are detected by gad eval preserve and logged as warnings.
decisions.impact: AGENTS.md updated for all 3 eval templates with mandatory layout section. gad eval preserve detects and warns on violations. DEFINITIONS.md documents the contract. Round 3 findings: GAD runs compliant (had .planning/), bare and emergent all violated by putting WORKFLOW.md and skills/ at project root. Round 4+ will enforce.

decisions.title: Eval preservation is a hard contract enforced by tests
decisions.summary: Every implementation eval run MUST end with these artifacts preserved at canonical locations: (1) evals/PROJECT/vN/TRACE.json, (2) evals/PROJECT/vN/run/ with code+planning, (3) apps/portfolio/public/evals/PROJECT/vN/ with the playable build, (4) evals/PROJECT/vN/.gad-log/ with CLI logs. The gad eval preserve command copies everything after a run; gad eval verify audits compliance; tests/eval-preservation.test.cjs fails the build if any run on or after the contract cutoff is missing artifacts. Before this decision, multiple runs' outputs were lost when worktrees were cleaned up or builds were overwritten.
decisions.impact: Future eval runs cannot be "forgotten" â€” the tests enforce preservation. Every agent running an eval must call gad eval preserve immediately after agent completion, before the worktree is removed. gad:eval-run and gad:eval-bootstrap skills updated with mandatory preservation step. DEFINITIONS.md documents the contract at the top.

decisions.title: Eval game builds must be preserved per-version, never overwritten
decisions.summary: Previously, gad eval open copied builds to apps/portfolio/public/evals/PROJECT/index.html, overwriting previous runs. This destroyed experimental history. Now all builds are preserved at apps/portfolio/public/evals/PROJECT/VERSION/index.html. The gad eval open command checks version-specific paths first.
decisions.impact: All 8 escape-the-dungeon builds now preserved (v5-v8, bare v1-v3, emergent v1-v2). Experiments can be compared across time. Showcase path is apps/portfolio/public/evals/PROJECT/VERSION/ for every run.

decisions.title: Freedom hypothesis: GAD constraint may correlate inversely with creative output quality
decisions.summary: Across 3 rounds of escape-the-dungeon evals, Bare (no framework) consistently outperformed GAD (full framework) on human review. Round 3: Bare v3 = 0.70, Emergent v2 = 0.50, GAD v8 = 0.20. GAD has never exceeded 0.30 across 4 attempts; Bare is monotonically improving (0.10 â†’ 0.50 â†’ 0.70). GAD's planning overhead may consume tokens that could go to implementation and testing. The process is followed but the product fails. Status: PRELIMINARY â€” single-run variance is high, needs more data. Confound: rate limits may have hit GAD harder because it was mid-planning.
decisions.impact: (1) Do not abandon GAD â€” this may be specific to greenfield game implementation. (2) Create a GAD-lite mode for comparison. (3) Add context-resumption eval where GAD should show its advantages. (4) Re-examine GAD's value proposition: is it optimizing for traceability/handoff rather than creative speed? (5) The game eval may be the wrong benchmark for GAD's core strengths.

decisions.title: Bare eval condition is a live contender, not a deprecated baseline
decisions.summary: Bare v2 scored 0.50 human review (most playable game) versus GAD v7 at 0.30. The bare condition responds well when informed by prior failure signals, making it a valid forward-looking experimental condition. All three conditions (GAD, Bare, Emergent) remain in active testing with equal priority.
decisions.impact: Do not deprioritize or drop the bare condition. Continue iterating on all three. The bare condition's strength is adaptation from failure feedback â€” test that hypothesis further.

decisions.title: Human review weight raised to 0.30 with low-score caps
decisions.summary: human_review weight increased from 0.15 to 0.30 in composite formula. Added hard caps: if human_review < 0.20, composite capped at 0.40; if < 0.10, capped at 0.25. This prevents technically compliant but broken/unusable output from ranking highly. Process metrics cannot override a bad user experience.
decisions.impact: Composite formula updated in DEFINITIONS.md and gad.cjs. All existing auto_composite scores are now provisional â€” human review is required for the real score.

decisions.title: Spell crafting is a gate criterion â€” loot/rewards do not satisfy it
decisions.summary: The player must intentionally combine runes/components through a forge/crafting interface and produce at least one new spell outcome. Pre-equipped spells or spell rewards from NPCs/loot do not count. Minimum: 3-5 runes, 3+ valid combinations, crafted spell usable in encounters.
decisions.impact: Gate G2 added to REQUIREMENTS.xml v3. No previous run has satisfied this â€” all runs will need to implement a forge system for the first time.

decisions.title: UI quality is a gate criterion at full presentation level
decisions.summary: The build must include icons, visually differentiated room presentation, styled controls/panels, and entity visual representation. Easily installable icon/sprite packs, web-searched assets, or generated placeholders are acceptable when setup cost is low. Raw ASCII/text-only UI fails the gate. Geometric fallback tiles are last resort, not primary path.
decisions.impact: All game eval REQUIREMENTS.xml updated to v3 with gate G3 (ui-quality). Agents must source visual assets as part of implementation. Asset sourcing can be a skill.

decisions.title: Inherited skills must capture failure modes and fixes, not just patterns
decisions.summary: Emergent v1 inherited 4 skills from bare v1 but still failed (KAPLAY styled text error). The skills documented code patterns but not failure modes. Bare v2 (told about v1 failure, no inherited skills) succeeded because it had to solve the problem itself. Skills that only document "how I did it" without "what went wrong and how I fixed it" are insufficient for cross-run learning.
decisions.impact: Skill format should include: pattern, when-to-use, failure-modes, and known-fixes sections. Previous-workflow.md should include specific error messages and resolutions, not just "it didn't work."

decisions.title: Eval requirements must be versioned and findings documented per experiment
decisions.summary: Requirements changed twice in one session (original 12 criteria â†’ gate criteria + vertical slice). Each change invalidates comparison with previous runs. Requirements must carry a version tag, and FINDINGS docs must reference which requirement version was used. Scientific rigor requires reproducibility.
decisions.impact: Add version field to REQUIREMENTS.xml. FINDINGS docs reference requirement version. TRACE.json includes requirements_version field.

decisions.title: Process metrics do not guarantee output quality â€” human review is the ground truth
decisions.summary: GAD v7 scored 0.81 discipline and 1.0 planning quality but only 0.30 human review (broken game loop). Bare v2 scored 0.0 discipline but 0.50 human review (most playable game). Auto-composite can be misleading â€” the 15% human_review weight is insufficient. For implementation evals that produce user-facing output, human review should be weighted higher or serve as a gate.
decisions.impact: Re-evaluate composite weights for game evals. Consider human_review as a multiplier rather than an additive dimension. A game that scores 0.0 human review should never have a composite above 0.50 regardless of process metrics.

decisions.title: Granular eval metrics: versioned schema, summary in TRACE, detail in optional metrics.json
decisions.summary: Automation must not depend on regex-parsing human-oriented `SCORE.md`. Implementation evals gain `scores.json` (or equivalent) alongside `SCORE.md` with stable numeric fields. Tooling evals store a summary composite (`tooling_composite` or nested `scores`) in `TRACE.json` for gates and trends; high-cardinality data (per-invocation latencies, histogram buckets, full event traces) go in `metrics.json` or retained JSONL only when needed. Bump `trace_schema_version` when the summary shape changes. `gad eval report` / `diff` read structured JSON, not markdown, for machine comparisons.
decisions.impact: score-generator and eval scores command updated to emit and read JSON. Report tables can aggregate tooling and implementation evals. Raw logs remain optional debug artifacts.

decisions.title: Tooling and MCP evals are automated, fixture-based, and comparable across versions
decisions.summary: Evaluating watch/dev and MCP differs from implementation evals (agents building projects). Tooling evals use `eval_type` of `tooling` or `mcp`, a fixed `scenario` id pointing at an in-repo fixture, and an automated harness (Node script or `gad eval tooling run`) that starts the watcher or MCP client, performs scripted actions, and asserts ground truth (missing paths, exit codes) plus latency metrics. No LLM in the loop. Each run writes `TRACE.json` with `trace_schema_version` so new granular fields (p50/p95, event counts, feature_flags) can be added without invalidating old runs. Compare runs with the same scenario id to measure improvement per feature or gad version.
decisions.impact: New eval projects under `evals/` for tooling/mcp. CI can run them on every PR touching gad.cjs or verify libs. Feature flags or gad version are recorded in the trace for attribution.

decisions.title: Real-time GAD tooling: core first, transports second
decisions.summary: The live developer loop (watch planning files, re-run refs verify, surface results quickly) is implemented once as a small core: debounced filesystem watch, call into existing verify code paths, emit structured events (JSON lines or NDJSON) for consumers. CLI (`gad dev` / `gad watch planning`) is the first transport. An MCP server, if added, is a thin adapter over the same core â€” not a separate implementation. Primary user is humans and local tooling (terminal, scripts, CI); agent use via MCP is optional and must not block shipping the core. IDE/editor-specific integrations (VS Code tasks, problem matchers, extensions) are out of scope until explicitly re-scoped â€” not required for the core.
decisions.impact: New watch/MCP code should live next to existing `gad refs` / verify libraries. No required network service for v1. MCP must not duplicate business logic.

decisions.title: All GAD CLI calls and agent tool uses are logged to JSONL for eval tracing
decisions.summary: Every gad CLI invocation logs to .planning/.gad-log/ (JSONL, one file per day): timestamp, command, args, duration, exit code. A PostToolUse hook (tool-trace.js) logs every agent tool call (Bash, Read, Write, Edit, Agent, Skill, Grep, Glob) to the same directory. During eval runs, GAD_LOG_DIR routes logs to the eval run directory for permanent storage. `gad log show` displays both log types interleaved with filters (--filter cli|tool|gad|skill|agent). `gad eval trace from-log` builds TRACE.json from actual logs (source: call-log) instead of git reconstruction. This is the definitive, numerical, measurable observability layer.
decisions.impact: Evals produce real call logs, not reconstructed approximations. Agents that spawn subagents, trigger skills, or call gad CLI commands are all traced. Log data enables: per-tool-call token estimates, skill trigger verification, gad CLI usage patterns, and cross-eval comparison on actual behavior.

decisions.title: All skills and agents must bootstrap via gad snapshot, not manual file reads
decisions.summary: Skills were hardcoding "read STATE.md, ROADMAP.md, DECISIONS.md, REQUIREMENTS.md" as Step 1 â€” 4+ file reads that the gad snapshot command already provides in one low-token output. Skills must use `gad snapshot --projectid <id>` as their bootstrap step. Individual CLI commands (gad tasks, gad decisions, gad phases) are available for drilling into detail. Skills must also be format-agnostic (XML or MD) per gad-06 â€” never hardcode .md or .xml extensions when referencing planning files. CLAUDE.md enforces this at the instance level: every fresh session runs gad snapshot first.
decisions.impact: All core skills updated: execute-phase, plan-phase, check-todos, quick. CLAUDE.md created as hard startup requirement. Subagent prompts should include the project ID so agents can run gad snapshot. Deprecated GSD compatibility notices removed from all skills (gad-19 already killed GSD).

decisions.title: Greenfield eval projects must auto-scaffold CONVENTIONS.md after phase 01
decisions.summary: The escape-the-dungeon agent created 13 source files with patterns (scene structure, content pack format, state management) but documented none of them. A follow-up agent would start cold with no conventions. After the first implementation phase, GAD should prompt or auto-generate a CONVENTIONS.md from the code patterns established â€” file structure, naming, import patterns, content pack format, scene registration pattern.
decisions.impact: Add a post-phase-01 checkpoint to eval-run and execute-phase that generates CONVENTIONS.md from the codebase. The gad:map-codebase skill already does this â€” it should be triggered automatically after the first implementation phase of any greenfield project.

decisions.title: Trace logging must be automatic â€” hooks or checkpoint-based, not manual log-cmd calls
decisions.summary: Manual trace logging (gad eval trace log-cmd/log-skill) requires the agent to remember to call them. The v2 eval proved this doesn't happen â€” the agent backfilled all 8 skill triggers in 15 seconds at the end. Tracing should be: (1) automatic via hooks that detect gad CLI calls and skill invocations, or (2) checkpoint-based where the agent must produce a trace entry to unlock the next phase, or (3) post-hoc by parsing git history and planning doc diffs to reconstruct what happened. Option 3 is the most reliable since it doesn't depend on agent cooperation.
decisions.impact: The eval-run workflow should reconstruct traces from git log + planning doc state changes, not rely on the agent to self-report. gad eval trace reconstruct --project <name> should parse git history to build TRACE.json after the fact.

decisions.title: Eval agents must be bootstrapped with GAD context â€” not just prompted
decisions.summary: The escape-the-dungeon eval (v2) showed that prompting an agent to "use gad snapshot" and "log traces" doesn't work â€” the agent ignored the CLI entirely, read requirements, and coded directly. Eval agents need: (1) GAD CLI available and working in the worktree, (2) AGENTS.md in the eval template that enforces the loop, (3) snapshot output injected into the agent's initial prompt (not "run this command"), (4) hooks or structured checkpoints that enforce task-level tracking. The agent must be inside the loop from the start, not asked to enter it.
decisions.impact: Eval templates must include an AGENTS.md with the GAD loop. The eval-run skill must inject snapshot context into the agent prompt. The agent's working environment must have gad CLI accessible. Honor-system tracing is insufficient â€” tracing must be automated or enforced via checkpoints.

decisions.title: Snapshot includes project-scoped conventions with global fallback
decisions.summary: gad snapshot includes a CONVENTIONS section: first checks project-level CONVENTIONS.md in .planning/, then extracts conventions/style sections from the project's AGENTS.md, then falls back to global root AGENTS.md conventions. Conventions grow per-project as code/content/patterns evolve â€” refactoring introduces new conventions, and the snapshot surfaces them to agents. Eval runs are expected to produce findings that tighten workflows, create new skills, and refine snapshot content.
decisions.impact: Every project can have its own CONVENTIONS.md. The snapshot surfaces the right conventions for the project being worked on. After eval runs, review outputs and create/update conventions based on what worked and what didn't.

decisions.title: GSD is dead â€” GAD and RepoPlanner only
decisions.summary: All GSD (get-shit-done) skills, hooks, commands, and references are removed from the repo. GAD is the planning framework. RepoPlanner provides the planning snapshot format that GAD consumes. No GSD commands in .claude/commands/, no GSD hooks in .claude/hooks/, no GSD references in settings.json or AGENTS.md. The context monitor and statusline hooks are useful infrastructure but must be rebranded for GAD â€” no npm checks for get-shit-done-cc.
decisions.impact: Delete .claude/commands/gsd/ entirely. Remove or rebrand .claude/hooks/gsd-* files. Clean settings.json. Remove GSD references from root AGENTS.md. Keep context monitor and statusline functionality but strip GSD branding.

decisions.title: The GAD loop is simple: snapshot â†’ work â†’ update docs â†’ commit
decisions.summary:
The canonical GAD loop for any agent, any coding tool:
1. `gad snapshot --projectid <id>` â€” one command, full context. No reading 15 files manually.
2. Pick one task from TASK-REGISTRY.xml (status=planned).
3. Implement it.
4. Update TASK-REGISTRY.xml (mark done), STATE.xml (next-action), DECISIONS.xml (if new decisions).
5. Commit.

That's it. AGENTS.md files in each project reference this loop but do NOT redefine it. The vendor/get-anything-done/ project is the canonical source. Root AGENTS.md and .planning/AGENTS.md are consumers that point here, not parallel definitions.

Skills are methodology documents (SKILL.md) â€” agents read and follow them. They are not CLI commands or executable plugins. The GAD CLI provides context efficiency. Skills provide methodology.
decisions.impact: Root AGENTS.md mandatory read order must be simplified. Agents should NOT read 15 files on startup â€” gad snapshot exists for this. AGENTS.md files across vendor projects reference the GAD loop, they don't each define their own version. No memory files, no shadow task lists â€” TASK-REGISTRY.xml and STATE.xml are the only task tracking.

decisions.title: Context exhaustion: work through auto-compact by default, suggest restart at clean boundaries when economically motivated
decisions.summary:
**Default behavior:** never stop work mid-task, never ask the user to restart, work through Claude Code's auto-compact event. After compaction, run `gad snapshot --projectid <id>` to re-hydrate and continue. The planning docs (STATE.xml next-action, TASK-REGISTRY.xml, DECISIONS.xml) are what make this possible â€” they are the durable state, not the conversation history.

**Refinement (2026-04-13):** The original "never suggest restart" rule is too absolute. There are real costs to letting a session grow indefinitely, and there are clean moments where a restart is the right call. The agent should *loosely* suggest restarts when the economics favor it.

**Cost shape of a long session:**
1. Every turn re-sends the entire conversation history as input tokens. Turn 100 pays for turns 1-99 again. Cost per turn grows linearly with session length.
2. Anthropic prompt cache has a 5-minute TTL. Idle gaps cold-start the cache and force a full re-read at full price.
3. As context fills, the model attends to more irrelevant history. Quality on the *current* task degrades even though the old turns are technically still there.
4. Context window is finite. Hitting the wall mid-task forces an unplanned auto-compact that is lossier than a planned restart.

**When a restart suggestion is appropriate:**
- A phase or large task just landed and its decisions are now captured in planning docs (rehydration story is good)
- The next chunk of work is clearly scoped and self-contained (no dangling in-head state)
- Topic is switching (e.g., "we're done with phase 43, now let's look at phase 44 marketplace design")
- The agent's context is past ~60-70% and the next task is independent
- Auto-compact is imminent â€” a planned restart now beats a forced compaction mid-task

**When a restart is NOT appropriate:**
- Mid-task with unwritten state in the agent's head
- During an investigation where the trail of "what we ruled out" carries weight
- When the next thing depends on subtle context from the last few turns
- Any time the user is in flow and a restart would interrupt their thinking

**How to suggest:** loosely, with one-line reasoning ("phase done, context ~70%, next chunk is mechanical and rehydratable from snapshot â€” restart is reasonable here, or I can keep going"). The user decides. Never push, never restart unilaterally. The default still leans toward continuing â€” restart is the exception, not the norm.
decisions.impact: Agents work through auto-compact by default and never restart unilaterally. They MAY suggest a restart loosely at clean planning boundaries when context is filling and the next chunk has a good rehydration story via `gad snapshot`. The user always decides. gad-04 (decisions in planning docs) is still what makes both auto-compact and planned restarts work â€” without durable docs, no rehydration story exists at all.

decisions.title: GAD does not support subagents until they are formally evaluated
decisions.summary: Spawning fresh agent instances (subagents via the Agent tool in Claude Code, or equivalent in other CLIs) is not part of the GAD workflow. Subagents start cold with no conversation history, burn context re-deriving state from files, introduce coordination overhead, and have uncharacterized failure modes. Until a formal GAD eval measures subagent utility against inline work, they are not a recommended tool. All work happens in a single agent session using the GAD planning loop.
decisions.impact: No GAD skill should instruct an agent to spawn subagents. The Agent tool is not referenced in any SKILL.md. When context limits are approached, the correct response is to finish the current task, update planning docs, commit, and start a fresh session â€” not to spawn a subagent mid-task.

decisions.title: portfolio-bare eval uses real agent sessions, not a harness â€” context_mode is self-declared
decisions.summary: The portfolio-bare eval is a real agent session in a template directory (evals/portfolio-bare/template/) containing only planning docs and requirements â€” no app code. The agent opens the template in Claude Code or Codex, runs `gad session new [--fresh]` to declare context mode, plans phases from requirements, executes tasks, and writes a trace at the end. No API keys, no execSync harness.
decisions.impact: The eval IS real agent work. context_delta emerges from comparing runs across context modes over time. A task that only appears in loaded runs is one the agent misses cold â€” the most actionable finding.

decisions.title: gad state and gad phases default to session project when a session is active
decisions.summary: When no --projectid flag is given, `gad state`, `gad phases`, `gad decisions`, `gad requirements`, `gad errors`, `gad blockers`, and `gad refs` call getActiveSessionProjectId() and scope output to the active session's project. Explicit --projectid overrides. --all bypasses session scope entirely and shows all roots. gad ls always shows all roots unscoped.
decisions.impact: Agents working in a session see only their project by default. Multi-project output via --all or when no session exists. token_reduction improved from 0.874 (v5) to 0.940 (v6).

decisions.title: TRACE.json is the machine-readable sidecar for every eval run
decisions.summary: Every eval run directory must contain a TRACE.json alongside RUN.md and SCORE.md. TRACE.json records: workflow (A/B/both), per-command token counts and unit fidelity, unit_coverage map (U1â€“U12 at Full/Referenced/Truncated/Approximated/Absent), and computed totals. Agents write it using `gad eval trace write` or inline during a run.
decisions.impact: Any new eval run must produce a TRACE.json. The diff command surfaces regressions and improvements per information unit. The report command shows persistent problem units across the full run history.

decisions.title: Missing-arg UX: print available choices and rerun hint, never a blind error
decisions.summary: All GAD CLI commands that require a --projectid or --id argument must, when absent, print the list of valid choices and a ready-to-run rerun hint on stderr before exiting 1. Blind citty "Missing required argument" errors are banned â€” required: true is replaced with our own check.
decisions.impact: Any future command added to gad.cjs that takes a required project/session/id arg must follow this pattern.

decisions.title: gad sink is the canonical docs sync command
decisions.summary: `gad sink` owns the four-direction docs sync surface: status (diff), compile (planningâ†’docs), sync (compile all, non-destructive), decompile (ensure dirs, create stubs), validate (mapping health). Sink is manual by default â€” no auto-compile on file change. Human-authored sink files (no generated: true frontmatter) are never overwritten.
decisions.impact: New code uses `gad sink compile` or `gad sink sync`. sink status shows: ok | stale | missing | human-authored. All 7 registered roots compile to the sink.

decisions.title: gad snapshot is the one-shot full project context command
decisions.summary: `gad snapshot` inlines every planning file in a project's .planning/ directory (excluding archive/, sessions/, node_modules/). Priority order: STATE, ROADMAP, REQUIREMENTS, DECISIONS, TASK-REGISTRY, then alphabetical. This is the canonical command for handing an agent a complete cold-start context pack. It differs from `gad context` â€” context gives session-aware minimal refs, snapshot gives everything.
decisions.impact: Agents starting a fresh session on an unfamiliar project use `gad snapshot --projectid <id>`. `gad context` is for ongoing work within a session.

decisions.title: gad context inlines file contents by default; --refs is the lightweight mode
decisions.summary: The `gad context` command inlines all planning file contents directly into its output by default. This gives agents a single command to get full context without reading files separately. The `--refs` flag restores the lightweight behavior (file paths only). This improves the completeness score on the cli-efficiency eval.
decisions.impact: Agents must use `gad context --refs` when they only want file paths. `gad context` without flags is verbose â€” suitable for session start, not for routine orientation checks.

decisions.title: GAD v1.0.0 defining characteristic: skills-first with evaluation
decisions.summary: Pre-1.0 (GSD/ralph-wiggum loop) was workflow-heavy â€” agents followed long workflow docs. v1.0.0 is skills-first: every skill has an agent-agnostic SKILL.md definition, is evaluated for output quality, and is versioned with the methodology. The CLI provides context efficiency. Evals are scientific. Traces measure what agents retrieve. Domain translators extend GAD to verticals.
decisions.impact: All new skills must have SKILL.md in vendor/get-anything-done/skills/. Skills are evaluated via the eval framework. Methodology version is tracked in package.json gadMethodologyVersion field.

decisions.title: gad-manuscript is a self-contained domain translator repo
decisions.summary: gad-manuscript is a standalone repo that defines the domain translator pattern â€” adapts GAD base methodology to book/fiction creation. It owns its own SKILL.md files, evals, and planning docs while referencing gad:* base skills. The magicborn in-world lore content in the docs sink is produced by gad-manuscript skills.
decisions.impact: Domain translator pattern is defined. Other verticals (product, design) follow the same pattern: standalone repo, own skills, own evals, references gad:* base. magicborn docs.project has content-skill = gad-manuscript.

decisions.title: GAD CLI is format-agnostic: XML, MD, MDX all valid in .planning/
decisions.summary: Sub-repos and .planning/ directories may use XML, MD, or MDX. The GAD CLI must parse all three transparently. The planning sink normalizes to MDX only. compile() converts any format to MDX; decompile() restores the original format. Coding agents and the CLI treat methodology as the constant â€” format is an implementation detail.
decisions.impact: gad sink validate checks parseability and round-trip fidelity, not format compliance. Any new project may choose its format. XML is preferred when both exist.

decisions.title: repo-planner is officially a GAD project; rp-* skills are deprecated
decisions.summary: vendor/repo-planner is registered as a GAD root (id=repo-planner) in planning-config.toml. It has a GAD-format .planning/ directory. Its skills (rp-*) are deprecated stubs pointing to gad:* equivalents. The manuscript skill content migrated to vendor/get-anything-done/skills/gad-manuscript/SKILL.md. The portfolio docs remain as the docs sink.
decisions.impact: gad ls shows both global and repo-planner. gad state, phases, and tasks all work for repo-planner. No new rp-* skills should be created.

decisions.title: All session intents, decisions, and guidance must be captured in planning docs each session
decisions.summary: Every working session that produces new decisions, requirements, or guidance must update DECISIONS.xml and the relevant planning docs before the session ends. Chat context is ephemeral â€” planning docs are the durable record. The GAD planning loop is the mechanism; this decision makes the capture requirement explicit.
decisions.impact: Agents starting a new session get current decisions from the planning docs, not from reconstructing chat history. Drift between chat intent and planning state is prevented.

decisions.title: Context = session-variable information units; static docs are exempt from CLI coverage
decisions.summary: Context for agent resumption is defined as the 10 session-variable information units (U1â€“U10) in DEFINITIONS.md. Static docs (AGENTS.md style guide, planning model reference tables, done-task history) do not constitute context loss if absent from CLI output â€” they are read once and cached. Context loss occurs only when a unit's absence would cause an agent to make a different next-action decision.
decisions.impact: Completeness scoring is now honest: units present at reduced fidelity (Truncated/Approximated) score 0.5, not 1.0. v3 completeness = 0.85, lower than v2's inflated 0.95.

decisions.title: Evals capture actual content, not just byte counts â€” token counts are the primary efficiency metric
decisions.summary: Every eval RUN.md must contain verbatim CLI and raw workflow outputs, a unit-by-unit fidelity table, token counts (chars/4), arithmetic showing all formulas applied, and an agent simulation proving the next-action decision is identical with and without the CLI. Byte counts alone are insufficient because they cannot reveal whether information is actually readable or complete.
decisions.impact: All future eval runs follow the scientific format defined in vendor/get-anything-done/evals/DEFINITIONS.md. v3 is the first run that meets this standard.

decisions.title: GAD is the universal planning framework â€” agent-agnostic, not Claude Code-specific
decisions.summary: GAD (get-anything-done) is the single planning framework for all projects in this repo. Skills are methodology documents (SKILL.md) stored in vendor/get-anything-done/skills/ â€” they must work with any AI coding assistant, not just Claude Code. Claude Code-specific install paths (~/.claude/) are implementation details, not the canonical source.
decisions.impact: All projects register under GAD via planning-config.toml. rp-* skills are deprecated; gad:* skills are canonical. No new framework-specific skill systems are added.

questions.title: Do eval worktrees actually inherit state from the parent monorepo, and does that contaminate the results?
questions.context: User concern 2026-04-09: 'a concern of mine when running the evals and rounds is that we might be polluting the experiments with our repo and monorepo installation.' Decision gad-82 commits us to eval isolation but does not yet verify whether Claude Code's worktree mechanism actually inherits parent-repo .claude/settings.json, skills, hooks, PATH, env vars. If it does, every eval score through round 4 may be contaminated by implicit access to GAD affordances the bare condition was not supposed to have. Isolation audit script queued as task 22-48. Round 5 is blocked on either passing the audit or documenting which parent-repo state is explicitly allowed.
questions.resolution: PASSES with explicit allowlist. Audit written at .planning/docs/ISOLATION-AUDIT-2026-04-10.md. Key findings: (1) git worktrees have separate working trees, so .agents/skills/ and .planning/ do NOT inherit — the bare condition stays clean of framework skills, (2) .claude/settings.json IS inherited by Claude Code worktrees at .claude/worktrees/agent-*/ (the settings search walks up), but this is acceptable because the only settings are the trace hook handler which is instrumentation not framework assistance, (3) gad eval run creates worktrees at os.tmpdir() which is entirely outside the repo — even cleaner. The secondary vector (globally-installed user skills at ~/.claude/skills/) is mitigated by adding a bare-eval-prompt line: 'Do NOT load or reference any globally-installed skills.' Round 5 is UNBLOCKED.

questions.title: Can we extract meaningful signal from Claude Code thinking blocks post-hoc?
questions.context: Per decision gad-84, Claude Code's hook system exposes tool calls but not thinking blocks or inter-tool message text. Those artifacts exist in session.jsonl. Open research question: can we extract them post-hoc and derive quality signal from them? Candidate metrics — thought-to-tool-use interval, thought length variance, ratio of 'exploration' to 'execution' phrases, thought-before-action vs thought-after-action ordering. All hypothetical until we have the extraction pipeline and look at real data. Feasibility assessment: high for extraction, unknown for signal.

questions.title: How do we compute a pressure score per eval operationally?
questions.context: Decision gad-75 names pressure as a first-class eval dimension with five sub-dimensions (requirement complexity, ambiguity, constraint density, iteration budget, failure cost). The dimensions are named; the formula is not. Open questions: (a) is pressure a single aggregate score or a 5-tuple? (b) is it self-rated by the requirements author or programmatically extracted? (c) if programmatic, which fields in REQUIREMENTS.xml feed which sub-dimension? (d) does pressure live on requirements (per version) or on runs (per execution), or both? (e) how do we validate that our pressure rating matches agent-experienced pressure — probably by correlating rating against tool_uses-to-failure ratio. Candidate first-pass: self-rated 0.0-1.0 per sub-dimension on each requirements version (stored in REQUIREMENTS-VERSIONS.md and in a new <pressure-profile> block in REQUIREMENTS.xml), aggregated as a weighted sum, displayed on the /roadmap timeline as a pressure-tier progression across rounds.

questions.title: Should pressure become a scored rubric dimension or stay as test-condition metadata?
questions.context: Pressure is CURRENTLY framed in gad-75 as metadata about the test conditions, not a measurement of the result. But there's a parallel interpretation: pressure could be scored — 'did the agent handle the pressure well'. That scoring collapses pressure into the existing rubric as a new dimension. The two framings answer different questions: metadata-pressure lets us normalize cross-round comparisons ('X scored 0.8 at pressure 0.6 vs Y scored 0.7 at pressure 0.9'), scored-pressure lets us rank agents on pressure-handling ability directly. Probably both are useful and we need both — one stored in REQUIREMENTS/TRACE metadata and one computed as a derived dimension. But committing to one framing first matters for the /roadmap page design and for any comparison visualization.

questions.title: Does a fresh clone of the repo actually work end-to-end for a human contributor?
questions.context: Per decision gad-77, before the /contribute page ships we need to verify: (a) cloning the repo into a new directory results in a working agent environment (skills in .claude/, agents installed, commands available), (b) opening the repo in Claude Code surfaces the GAD skills to the agent without additional setup, (c) a conversational request like 'run the escape-the-dungeon-bare eval' actually works with no manual snapshot or XML editing. This is an untested assumption right now — we've been running agents in the canonical development repo where everything is already wired up. A fresh clone might hit missing .claude/settings.json entries, missing hook handler paths, missing env vars, or installer bugs. Until this is tested, /contribute is vaporware.

questions.title: Do we already have create-skill / merge-skill / find-skills as fundamentals, and if not, should we build them?
questions.context: User's vision (decision gad-73): GAD provides three fundamental skills as the foundation of emergent evolution. find-skills locates a trusted GAD fundamental (e.g. 'scientific method', 'debug'). merge-skill fuses that fundamental into a project-tailored skill (e.g. 'scientific-method-for-kaplay-rune-spellcrafting'). create-skill authors genuinely new ones when no merge candidate exists. This triumvirate IS the in-game rune/spell merging mechanic made meta. We need to audit what exists today in skills/ — create-skill likely already exists, merge-skill and find-skills may not. Reference: https://skills.sh/vercel-labs/skills/find-skills. GAD's version scopes to trusted ecosystem initially. Bigger ambition: prove skill effectiveness + provide skill security.

questions.title: Should the landing page stop leading with 'ship software faster' and lead with task-management + skill evaluation instead?
questions.context: Per decision gad-74, GAD's actual value is not 'ship production software faster' — the freedom hypothesis already shows bare can outperform on creative implementation. Real value: (1) durable task management at scale with everything in-repo (no external DB, no lock-in), (2) scientific framework for proving skill effectiveness + workflow discipline, (3) skill security posture (longer-term). Landing page should reframe around these. Current landing hero is implicitly still about 'use GAD to build things' — it should be 'use GAD to understand whether agents are actually building things well.'
questions.resolution: Resolved by decision gad-76 and the landing rewrite shipped in the 2026-04-09 IA session. New value-prop line: 'A system for evaluating and evolving agents through real tasks, measurable pressure, and iteration.' Landing primary CTA is now Play (B), above-the-fold stack is Play → Methodology → Findings → Hypothesis → Fork. Target audience is primarily coding-agent researchers (A) with indie devs (C) as experiential secondary entry. Pressure (gad-75) is introduced as the hook that differentiates GAD from other coding-agent frameworks.

questions.title: What does 'skill security' actually look like in practice?
questions.context: User wants GAD to eventually provide a trust model for skills — how can you tell if a skill you're about to inherit is safe, effective, and actually improves anything? Initial thoughts: (a) frontmatter signing / checksum, (b) provenance lineage (which run authored it, which rubric scores validated it, which other runs inherited it successfully), (c) automated review against Anthropic's skills guide (gad-70), (d) sandboxed trial run in a throwaway worktree before trusting. Distinct from typosquatting defense (which lives in the planned /security page). This is about effectiveness + integrity, not name collisions.

questions.title: Does compounding skill inheritance (CSH) eventually beat raw freedom (freedom hypothesis) across many rounds?
questions.context: Round 4 produced a provocative finding: Emergent v4 scored 0.885 (with the 6-dim rubric including skill_inheritance_effectiveness), Bare v5 scored 0.805 (5-dim rubric), and GAD v10 was api-interrupted. On shared rubric dimensions (playability, ui_polish, mechanics, ingenuity, stability) Emergent beat Bare on playability and UI polish, tied on mechanics/ingenuity/stability. The freedom hypothesis (gad-36) says bare beats framework on creative output. The compound-skills hypothesis (gad-65) says emergent-with-evolution compounds over rounds. Round 4 may be the first evidence that CSH is overtaking freedom — BUT it's one round, rubric reweighting matters, and Emergent inherited from Bare so it has freedom-hypothesis lineage baked in. Need more rounds against v5 requirements to disambiguate.

questions.title: Which of our current eval metrics should become programmatic first?
questions.context: Per gad-69, every eval metric must answer 'can this be collected programmatically?' before 'how do we score it?'. We currently rely heavily on human review + agent self-report for: did the skill actually load, did the gate pass, is the forge integrated with pressure, is the game beatable. A programmatic-eval GAPS audit is queued as task 83 / task 96. The output should be .planning/GAPS.md ranking gaps by (a) how much human/agent judgment they currently require, (b) how mechanically checkable they actually are, (c) which phase picks them up. Highest candidates: playwright smoke tests for G1-G4 gates, hook-captured skill-trigger events, build/test exit codes as stability signals.

questions.title: Should v5 mandate Unicorn-Overlord-style rule-based combat (Model A) or allow direct-control (Model B)?
questions.context: Bare v5 playtest surfaced a preference for rule-based simulated combat (loadout + spells + stats + action policies + initiative, chess-like positioning) over direct-control. R-v5.13 captures this as 'Model A preferred unless implementation exception granted.' Open question: is this the right call for round 5? Rule-based is harder to implement correctly in a single eval run and may advantage GAD's planning overhead (which would invalidate the freedom comparison if it's the reason bare underperforms next round).

questions.title: How do we measure the value of authored-content-pack inheritance (gad-66) without confounding freedom/CSH tests?
questions.context: Decision gad-66 proposes an eval flavor that inherits pre-authored content (spells, runes, items, NPCs) alongside requirements. The user's goal is a 'really complex game made with all the builds and data around it.' But injecting content into a CSH-testing run would confound the compound-skills signal because the agent didn't author the content. Proposal: run it as a separate eval track (escape-the-dungeon-inherited-content) distinct from greenfield emergent, so CSH measurements stay clean. Still open: how do we compare a content-pack run to a greenfield run fairly?
questions.resolution: Resolved: content-pack injection becomes its own eval track (separate from greenfield emergent) so CSH measurements stay clean. User reframed it as a *content-driven hypothesis* — analogous to making a game or movie derivative from a book. It is explicitly derivative work: not all processes are, 'much like a forger might not use the exact same brush.' This is a distinct hypothesis from freedom and CSH, with its own track, its own rounds, and its own comparison rules. We do NOT compare content-pack runs to greenfield runs on the same rubric — they answer different questions. The content-pack track's scoring focuses on: (a) does the extra content produce a more fleshed-out game given the same token budget, (b) does the agent integrate the content coherently rather than just bolting it on, (c) does the final game feel unified despite derivative source material.

questions.title: What is the actual root cause of the HTTP 529 overloaded_error crashing GAD runs?
questions.context: GAD v10 was api-interrupted twice by HTTP 529 (once at tool_uses=18, then 55). This is distinct from account rate limits (gad-64). Per STATE.xml, investigation is queued before GAD v11 retry. Without understanding whether 529 correlates with high planning overhead (GAD-specific), payload size, tool-use frequency, or time of day, we can't plan round 5 reliably.

questions.title: Who exactly is the site + framework for?
questions.context: Task 90 queues ASSUMPTIONS.md. Without a documented target user, IA refactor decisions (task 84), skills directory UX (task 85), and landing-page framing will be ad-hoc. Candidates: coding-agent researchers, framework authors, indie devs exploring skill evolution, enterprise teams evaluating agent frameworks. Each implies a different navigation priority.

questions.title: Is the 6th skill_inheritance_effectiveness dimension on emergent an unfair boost vs the 5-dim bare/gad rubric?
questions.context: Emergent v4 aggregate: 0.885. Bare v5 aggregate: 0.805. The 0.08 delta maps almost exactly to emergent's 6th-dimension contribution (skill_inheritance_effectiveness 0.95 * 0.20 weight = +0.19 against bare's absent-dimension 0). Shared-dimension comparison: emergent +0.18 playability, +0.10 ui_polish, tie on mechanics/ingenuity/stability. Is that 6th dimension double-counting emergent's inheritance advantage? Arguably yes — CSH is being tested by the existence of that dimension. Counter: without it, there's no way to score whether inheritance is actually working, which is the whole point of the emergent condition.

questions.title: How do we keep the site navigable as we add /security, /glossary, /roadmap, /skills-guide, /questions, /compare?
questions.context: Current nav already has 11 items (GAD, Lineage, Methodology, Rubric, Results, Graphs, Videos, Catalog, Findings, Planning, and now + Rubric). Tasks 86/87/88/95 will add more. Task 84 plans dropdown grouping + keyword search before more pages ship. Open: what is the grouping that makes sense to a first-time visitor vs a returning researcher?

bugs.title: Rune forge allows same rune twice in a single spell, boosts affinity twice
bugs.description: In the rune forge, the player can select the same rune twice as ingredients for a single spell. When crafted, the affinity gain for that rune is applied twice as if two distinct rune slots were consumed. The resulting spell also treats the duplicate as a meaningful second ingredient.
bugs.expected: Selecting a rune that is already in the current spell's ingredient list should either reject the second click or toggle that rune off. Affinity gain should be per-unique-rune-slot. Duplicate runes across DIFFERENT spells in the player's spellbook remain fine and are explicitly allowed.
bugs.reproduction: Open forge. Select Fire rune. Select Fire rune again. Craft. Observe that Fire affinity increases by 2x the normal amount for a single-rune craft.

bugs.title: Toast notifications persist above the screen across new game sessions
bugs.description: Notifications like 'trained fire affinity' appear and never auto-dismiss. Starting a new game does not clear the backlog; they remain above the latest build's UI.
bugs.expected: Every notification auto-dismisses after a short timeout (3-5s) or has an explicit close control. Starting a new game clears the notification queue. Page reload clears stale notifications.
bugs.reproduction: Play until a notification appears. Do not dismiss. Start a new game. Observe notification still overlays the new session.

bugs.title: Continue button disabled after death — no save checkpoints
bugs.description: On player death the game ends and Continue is no longer selectable. No automatic checkpoint appears to have been created during the run.
bugs.expected: Room-clear or floor-clear creates a checkpoint. On death, Continue loads the most recent checkpoint.
bugs.reproduction: Start new game. Die in any encounter. Return to title. Continue is greyed out.

bugs.title: Glitchy redraws on button clicks across all round-4 builds
bugs.description: UI visibly glitches/flickers on button clicks as if full per-tick redraws are running. Observed consistently across GAD v9, GAD v10, Bare v5, and Emergent v4.
bugs.expected: Event-driven rendering only. No redraw loops firing at a fixed rate regardless of state changes. React/DOM reconciles on state change; requestAnimationFrame only runs when animation is active.
bugs.reproduction: Click any menu button in any round-4 build. Observe brief UI flicker.

glossary.short: The working hypothesis that a coding agent's skill library compounds in value over many rounds as skills are merged and tailored to a specific project — like a master craftsman accumulating tools for their craft.
glossary.full: The Compound-Skills Hypothesis says the **emergent** eval condition should produce monotonically improving results as its inherited skill library grows more specialized to the project domain. The craftsman metaphor: a blacksmith who learns one skill per hour and authors one skill per hour eventually becomes a master of the specific kind of blade they keep forging. Signals we track: (1) human review score increases across rounds, (2) inherited skill count grows, (3) ratio of project-specific skills to generic methodology skills shifts toward specific, (4) time-to-working-game decreases as requirements get stricter. **Distinct from the freedom hypothesis** — CSH is about compounding returns over time, not about bare vs framework in a single round. First concrete evidence observed in round 4 with emergent v4 authoring `dom-over-kaplay` and `pressure-forge-coupling` skills while deprecating `kaplay-scene-pattern`.

glossary.short: For creative implementation tasks, agent performance correlates INVERSELY with framework constraint — less prescribed structure leads to better output.
glossary.full: The Freedom Hypothesis emerged from round 3's finding that the bare eval condition (no framework) outperformed the GAD condition (full framework) on human review scores across multiple rounds. Supporting evidence: Bare has improved monotonically 0.10 → 0.50 → 0.70 → 0.805 across rounds 2-4. GAD has never exceeded 0.30 human review across its first four attempts. Interpretation: GAD's planning overhead consumes tokens that could have gone to testing and fixing the game. The hypothesis is **preliminary** — single-run variance is high and framework overhead may pay off on different benchmarks (resumption after compaction, multi-phase refactors, compliance work). The Compound-Skills Hypothesis was formulated partly as a response to this finding: maybe the framework compounds over rounds in ways a single round can't show.

glossary.short: Synthesis of freedom + compound-skills + project-tailored merging. Projects are themselves emergent; skills must be tailored, merged, and rehearsed against a foundational pool.
glossary.full: Formal statement: a coding agent given (a) blank planning artifacts, (b) requirements, (c) the task, and (d) the ability to create, merge, and find skills from a GAD-provided foundational pool will produce better work than framework-constrained or bare conditions over time — because projects are themselves emergent, and skills must be tailored continually and merged with similar foundational skills to maintain consistency. The craftsman/lifter metaphor: a master lifter varies exercise and lowers weight to stay sharp, a craftsman returns to fundamentals to maintain form. GAD's role under this framing is NOT to prescribe process — it's to provide the foundational skill pool and an evolution substrate. This is the single most important research direction going forward.

glossary.short: An eval condition where the agent has no mandated framework but inherits skills from previous runs, evolves them in place, and writes a CHANGELOG.
glossary.full: The emergent workflow is one of three eval conditions (alongside GAD and bare). The agent runs without a prescribed framework but starts with a skill library inherited from previous bare or emergent runs on the same project. The agent is allowed (and encouraged) to update, deprecate, or author new skills based on what it learned. A CHANGELOG.md tracks the disposition of every inherited skill: kept-as-is, evolved, deprecated-as-wrong, or replaced-by-new. This is the workflow that produces evidence for the Compound-Skills Hypothesis. First full ratcheting cycle observed in round 4 with emergent v4 authoring two new skills and deprecating one.

glossary.short: An eval condition where the agent has no framework and no inherited skills — only AGENTS.md, requirements, and freedom.
glossary.full: The bare workflow is the simplest eval condition. The agent is given only requirements and an AGENTS.md directive to keep workflow artifacts under `game/.planning/` — nothing else. No framework, no skill library, no prescribed process. It exists to test the Freedom Hypothesis: can an unconstrained agent outperform a framework-driven one on creative implementation? As of round 4, the answer has been yes on human review scores, though the Emergent workflow has recently pulled ahead with its inherited skill library (round 4 emergent 0.885 vs bare 0.805).

glossary.short: An eval condition where the agent uses the full GAD framework — .planning/ XML, AGENTS.md loop, skill triggers, plan → execute → verify → commit.
glossary.full: The GAD workflow is the framework-driven eval condition. The agent follows the full GAD planning loop: read STATE, pick a task, implement, update planning docs, commit. It has access to the entire GAD skill library, the subagent system, and formal phase/task tracking. Across rounds 1-4, GAD has consistently produced high process discipline (planning quality ~1.0, commits-per-task ~1.0) but low human-review scores (never exceeded 0.30). This gap between process metrics and output quality led to the Freedom Hypothesis and is the core puzzle the Emergent-Evolution Hypothesis is trying to resolve.

glossary.short: An eval mode where the agent builds a project from nothing — no existing codebase, just requirements and a starting template.
glossary.full: Greenfield is one of two eval modes (the other is brownfield). In greenfield mode, the agent starts with essentially nothing: a blank repo, a requirements document, and (depending on the workflow) either a full framework, a bare AGENTS.md, or an inherited skill library. The escape-the-dungeon game evals are all greenfield — each run builds the game from scratch. Greenfield tests the agent's ability to design and implement from zero. Compare with brownfield, which tests the agent's ability to extend and improve an existing codebase.

glossary.short: An eval mode where the agent inherits an existing codebase baseline and must extend or improve it against new requirements.
glossary.full: Brownfield is one of two eval modes (the other is greenfield). In brownfield mode, the eval starts with a baseline codebase — typically the output of a prior greenfield run — and asks the agent to extend it against new or expanded requirements. This tests a different set of skills: code archaeology, maintaining consistency with existing patterns, understanding why something was done before changing it. The brownfield etd-brownfield-gad/bare/emergent projects inherit bare v3 as their baseline. Brownfield is where framework-driven workflows (GAD) might show their value — long-term maintenance rather than one-shot creative output.

glossary.short: A binary pass/fail requirement that, if failed, zeroes the requirement_coverage score regardless of other criteria met.
glossary.full: Gates are the load-bearing requirements every run must pass to be considered a serious attempt. Requirements v3 introduced four gates: G1 game-loop (full cycle title → new game → room → combat → return → continue), G2 forge-with-ingenuity-payoff (spell crafting exists AND at least one encounter per floor significantly favors crafted spells), G3 ui-quality (no raw ASCII, icons/bars/spacing intentional), G4 pressure-mechanics (at least 2 of resource/enemy/encounter/build pressure present). If any gate fails, requirement_coverage = 0 and the composite score is capped. Gates exist because automated requirement counting can hit 0.95 while the actual game is a blank screen.

glossary.short: A set of weighted dimensions a human reviewer scores 0.0 – 1.0 independently, summed to produce human_review.score.
glossary.full: The rubric replaces a single-number human review score with a per-dimension breakdown. Each eval project defines its own rubric in `gad.json` under `human_review_rubric`. Dimensions include playability, ui_polish, mechanics_implementation, ingenuity_requirement_met, stability. The Emergent workflow gets a sixth dimension, skill_inheritance_effectiveness, that specifically tests the Compound-Skills Hypothesis. A reviewer submits scores via `gad eval review <project> v<N> --rubric '{...}' --notes '...'`. The weighted aggregate becomes `human_review.score` in the composite formula. See the /rubric page for each project's full rubric.

glossary.short: Weighted sum of dimension scores per project. Each project weights dimensions differently. Range 0.0 – 1.0.
glossary.full: Composite is the single derived number representing a run's overall quality. It's a weighted sum over a project's scoring dimensions — the weights live in `gad.json` under `scoring.weights`. Different projects weight differently: a tooling eval might weight time_efficiency highly; an implementation eval weights human_review at 0.30 to prevent process-metric-only wins. Gate failures cap the composite by zeroing requirement_coverage. Low-score caps: composite < 0.20 → 0.40, composite < 0.10 → 0.25. The composite is displayed on every run card and per-run page.

glossary.short: The v4 principle that baseline starter abilities must NOT be sufficient to complete a floor — the player must adapt, craft, or specialize.
glossary.full: Ingenuity is the core design principle of requirements v4. Every mechanical system must create friction that rewards creative player choice. If a player can brute-force a floor using only default abilities, the design has failed. The G2 forge gate expanded in v4 from 'crafting exists' to 'at least one encounter per floor significantly favors crafted/adapted spells.' In the rubric, ingenuity_requirement_met is a scored dimension that asks whether the player was FORCED to think. This is why round 4 surfaced the observation that Emergent v4's floor-1 is 'beatable only by skipping combat, gathering mana, crafting the right spells, then fighting the boss fresh' — that's ingenuity working as intended.

glossary.short: The in-game spell-crafting interface where players combine runes (and eventually spells) to create new abilities.
glossary.full: The rune forge is the escape-the-dungeon spell-crafting system and the centerpiece of the G2 gate. Players select runes, combine them, and produce new spells. Requirements v4 requires at least 3-5 runes, 3+ valid combinations, and crucially, at least one encounter per floor where the crafted spell provides a meaningful advantage. v5 (addendum) expands the forge: spells themselves become craftable ingredients (R-v5.19), rune uniqueness per spell is enforced (R-v5.20), and affinity is gained through casting not menu training (R-v5.01). The forge is also an explicit in-game analogue for the GAD skill-merging meta-loop — merging runes into spells mirrors merging fundamental skills into project-tailored ones.

glossary.short: A per-rune progression stat gained by using that rune — higher affinity means stronger spells using it.
glossary.full: Rune affinity is how players specialize their magical build in escape-the-dungeon. In v4 and earlier, affinity gain was implementation-dependent; in v5 (addendum R-v5.01), affinity must be gained as a side effect of casting spells in encounters — not via a training menu. A training-dummy encounter room type provides a low-risk place to cast without consequence. v5 also requires a visible affinity reward loop (R-v5.16): boosting a rune's affinity must produce a visible, valuable payoff (new rune variant, spell tier, gated event access). Affinity is a build-pressure mechanic per G4.

glossary.short: Systems that constrain player behavior so crafting or adapting spells provides a meaningful advantage.
glossary.full: Pressure mechanics are the v4 G4 gate. A passing game must include at least two of: (a) resource pressure — limited mana, limited healing, forge cost; (b) enemy counterplay — resistances, immunities, behavior that punishes repetition; (c) encounter design pressure — elites, bosses that invalidate default play, rooms with mechanical constraints; (d) build pressure — affinity pushes specialization, loadout limits force trade-offs. At least one pressure mechanic must interact with the forge so crafted spells are demonstrably more effective than defaults. Pressure is what makes the Ingenuity requirement bite — without pressure, ingenuity is optional.

glossary.short: The current version of TRACE.json plus hook-captured event streams. Every tool use, skill invocation, subagent spawn, and file mutation is recorded.
glossary.full: Trace schema v4 is the instrumentation layer that makes programmatic evaluation possible. Every run produces a TRACE.json file with summary metrics plus a stream of events captured via Claude Code PreToolUse/PostToolUse hooks. Event types: tool_use, skill_invocation, subagent_spawn, file_mutation. Event output fields are capped at 4 KB with head/tail truncation; full output stays in Claude Code's session.jsonl for deeper inspection. The framework version is stamped on every TRACE so evals can be re-run across framework versions without contamination. All the programmatic-eval gaps in GAPS.md are built on top of this event stream.
